request.question: what year was the 2008 electee for a Cheshire constituency represented in the UK Parliament born?

title: Dartford (UK Parliament constituency)
North Wiltshire (UK Parliament constituency)
John Lloyd Wharton
Pendle (UK Parliament constituency)
Crewe and Nantwich (UK Parliament constituency)
Harrogate (UK Parliament constituency)
West Suffolk (UK Parliament constituency)
Stalybridge (UK Parliament constituency)
Edward Timpson
Mansfield (UK Parliament constituency)
sentences: Dartford is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Gareth Johnson of the Conservative Party.
The constituency is currently the longest-valid 'bellwether' constituency in the country as the party of the winning candidate has gone on to form the government in every General Election since 1964.
Candidates for the largest two parties nationally have polled first and second since 1923 in Dartford.
North Wiltshire is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 1997 by James Gray, a Conservative.

In the period 1832–1983, this was an alternative name for Chippenham or the Northern Division of Wiltshire and as Chippenham dates to the original countrywide Parliament, the Model Parliament, this period is covered in more detail in that article.
In 2016 it was announced that the North Wiltshire constituency would be scrapped as part of the planned 2018 Constituency Reforms.
The Right Honourable John Lloyd Wharton PC (18 April 1837 – 11 July 1912) was a Barrister and a Conservative Party politician.
He was the Member of Parliament (MP) for Member of Parliament (MP) for City of Durham (UK Parliament constituency) then Member of Parliament (MP) for Ripon (UK Parliament constituency).
Pendle is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Andrew Stephenson, a Conservative.
The constituency was newly created for the 1983 General Election, being largely formed from the former Nelson and Colne Constituency.
Crewe and Nantwich is a constituency in Cheshire represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2017, by Laura Smith of the Labour Party.
Harrogate ( ) was a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament.
As with all constituencies, the constituency elected one Member of Parliament (MP) by the first past the post system of election.
The constituency was renamed Harrogate and Knaresborough in 1997.
West Suffolk is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Matthew Hancock, a Conservative.
Between 1832 and 1885 there had also been a constituency, the Western Division of Suffolk that had also been called West Suffolk.
Stalybridge officially sometimes written in early years as Staleybridge was a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament from 1868 until 1918 by one MP.
It comprised the borough of Stalybridge which lay in Lancashire and Cheshire and which is in the east of today's Greater Manchester.
On abolition for the 1918 general election under the Representation of the People Act 1918 the seat's main replacement became Stalybridge and Hyde.
Anthony Edward Timpson (born (1973--)26 1973 ) is a British Conservative Party politician.
He was first elected as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Crewe and Nantwich at the 2008 by-election but lost his seat in the 2017 general election to the Labour candidate, Laura Smith.
Mansfield is a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2017 by Ben Bradley of the Conservative Party.
This is the first time a Conservative MP has represented the constituency since its creation in 1885.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: what year was the 2008 electee for a Cheshire constituency represented in the UK Parliament born?
hop1_docs: Eddisbury (UK Parliament constituency) | Eddisbury is a constituency in Cheshire represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2015 by Antoinette Sandbach, a Conservative.
Congleton (UK Parliament constituency) | Congleton is a constituency in Cheshire, represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2010 by Fiona Bruce of the Conservative Party.
Halton (UK Parliament constituency) | Halton is a constituency in Cheshire, represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 1997 by Derek Twigg of the Labour Party.

Crewe and Nantwich (UK Parliament constituency) | Crewe and Nantwich is a constituency in Cheshire represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament since 2017, by Laura Smith of the Labour Party.

Cheshire (UK Parliament constituency) | Cheshire is a former United Kingdom Parliamentiary constituency for the county of Cheshire.  It was a constituency of the House of Commons of the Parliament of England then of the Parliament of Great Britain from 1707 to 1800 and of the Parliament of the United Kingdom from 1801 to 1832.

Stalybridge (UK Parliament constituency) | Stalybridge officially sometimes written in early years as Staleybridge was a constituency represented in the House of Commons of the UK Parliament from 1868 until 1918 by one MP.  It comprised the borough of Stalybridge which lay in Lancashire and Cheshire and which is in the east of today's Greater Manchester.  On abolition for the 1918 general election under the Representation of the People Act 1918 the seat's main replacement became Stalybridge and Hyde.

Hyde (UK Parliament constituency) | Hyde was a county constituency represented in the House of Commons of the Parliament of the United Kingdom from 1885 until 1918.  It was seated in the town of Hyde, Cheshire.
response.hop2_query:
SELECT ?person ?personLabel ?birthYear WHERE {
  # Person held the position "Member of Parliament"
  ?person p:P39 ?posStmt.
  ?posStmt ps:P39 wd:Q16707842.

  # The statement has an electoral district (constituency) and a term start in 2008
  ?posStmt pq:P768 ?constituency.
  ?posStmt pq:P580 ?startTime.
  FILTER(YEAR(?startTime) = 2008)

  # The constituency is in an area labeled "Cheshire"
  ?constituency wdt:P131 ?area.
  ?area rdfs:label "Cheshire"@en.

  # Get birth year
  ?person wdt:P569 ?birthDate.
  BIND(YEAR(?birthDate) AS ?birthYear)

  SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language "en". }
}
LIMIT 50
hop2_docs: Constituencies of Belize | Belize's 6 districts are politically divided into 31 constituencies.  Each constituency sends one representative to Belize's House of Representatives for 5-year terms.  This election is known as the General Election.  Each person (who is eligible to vote) votes for the candidate they would want to represent their constituency in Central Government.

List of Parliamentary constituencies in Cheshire | The ceremonial county of Cheshire (which includes the areas of the Cheshire West and Chester, Cheshire East, Halton and Warrington unitary authorities) is divided into 11 Parliamentary constituencies.  The 2 divisions of Warrington are Borough constituencies,

Constituency Commission | The Constituency Commission (Irish: "An Coimisiún um Thoghlaigh" ) is an independent commission in Ireland, which advises on redrawing of constituency boundaries for election of members to Dáil Éireann (the lower house of the Oireachtas, the national parliament) and the European Parliament.  Each commission comes into being after the census, submits a non-binding report to the Oireachtas, and is dissolved.  A separate but similar Local Electoral Area Boundary Committee fulfils the same function for Local Electoral Area boundaries.

General elections in Singapore | General elections in Singapore must be held within three months after five years have elapsed from the date of the first sitting of a particular Parliament of Singapore.  However, in most cases Parliament is dissolved and a general election called at the behest of the Prime Minister before the five-year period elapses.  The number of constituencies or electoral divisions is not permanently fixed by law, but is declared by the Prime Minister prior to each general election pursuant to the Parliamentary Elections Act (Cap.  218, 2011 Rev. Ed. ) , which governs the conduct of elections to Parliament, taking into account recommendations of the Electoral Boundaries Review Committee.  For the 2015 general election, there were 89 seats in Parliament organised into 13 Single Member Constituencies (SMCs) and 16 Group Representation Constituencies (GRCs).  Each SMC returns one Member of Parliament while each GRC returns between three and six MPs, at least one of whom must be from the Malay, Indian or other minority communities.  A group of persons wishing to stand for election in a GRC must all be members of the same political party, or a group of independent candidates.  The voting age in Singapore is 21 years.

House of Assembly of Saint Lucia | The Parliament of Saint Lucia has two chambers.  The House of Assembly is the popular elected chamber of the Legislature.  It has 17 or 18 members: 17 members elected for a five-year term in single-seat constituencies, and a speaker elected by the 17 members, who may be one of their number or a person chosen from outside the House.

Elections in Uganda | Uganda provides national elections for a president and a legislature.  The president is elected for a five-year term.  The Parliament is composed of members directly elected to represent constituencies, and one woman representative for every district; as well representatives of special interest groups, including the army, youth, workers and persons with disabilities.

East Cheshire (UK Parliament constituency) | East Cheshire was parliamentary constituency which returned two Members of Parliament (MPs) to the House of Commons of the Parliament of the United Kingdom.  Elections were held using the bloc vote system.
